CHARTS / BASKETS
% RETURN, REBASED · EOD 2026-06-15
Rotation ReadCitrini · claude

Semis and AI infrastructure have broken away from the megacap platform layer, and that is the defining rotation this month. Semis averaged +28.5% over the past month, AI infra +27.9%, with ARM leading hardware and MRVL running point in chips. Mag 7 fell -4.3% over the same window, dragged by GOOGL. XLC (communications) is down -4.12% this month and -4.05% YTD. Quantum stocks averaged +29.9% this month, a sharp sentiment reversal from a -1.1% YTD baseline, confirming the bid is chasing anything silicon-adjacent. You own the silicon supply chain. You avoid the application layer.

Energy is the month's other big story. XLE lost -8.88% in one month despite entering the period up +20.92% YTD. The commodity trade is reversing hard. Materials (XLB +5.68%) and Industrials (XLI +5.17%) held their gains, telling you this is crude-specific, not a macro demand scare. Banks (+4.17%) and Healthcare (+4.41%) joined the advance. Participation is widening into AI-adjacent cyclicals and away from pure commodities.

The factor picture seals the read. MTUM/SPY sits in the top 1% of its yearly range, SPHB/SPY in the top 1% as well. You are paid to own beta and momentum in the right pockets. XLC/SPY is in the bottom 1% of its yearly range. EWT/SPY (Taiwan, the heart of the semi supply chain) is up +48.42% YTD. The intra-sector split is everything here: the sub-industry building the hardware is winning, and the sub-industry deploying it at the platform level is losing.

Semis and AI hardware are ripping while Mag 7 bleeds and Energy collapses.

BULL

The computation build-out is accelerating. Semis up +28.5% and AI infra +27.9% this month confirm real capex demand, not narrative. Materials and Industrials are broadening the cyclical base, reducing the single-point-of-failure risk.

BEAR

Mag 7 down -4.3% and XLC in the bottom 1% of its yearly range signal the application layer has stopped responding to the hardware rally. If megacap platform tech does not catch up, the AI trade runs on one leg.

TELL

XLC/SPY relative strength. If comms stabilizes and GOOGL starts participating, the Mag 7 lag becomes a catch-up trade. If XLC keeps making new relative lows, the hardware rally is building on a narrowing base.

⚠ Value factor (VLUE/SPY at **z=2.68**, in the top **1%** of its yearly range) is running alongside growth (XLK/SPY at **z=3.19**, top **2%**). vs Value and growth factors typically diverge. Both cannot be outperforming in a zero-sum factor world. — The AI capex cycle is the shared catalyst: banks and materials (value-heavy) benefit from infrastructure financing and commodity demand, while semis drive the growth read. Both can hold simultaneously when one dominant theme drives the cycle. Trust the growth read as the primary thesis; the value broadening is real but secondary.
⚠ SPHB/SPLV at **z=2.66** (top **2%** of its yearly range) shows high beta crushing low vol, implying broad risk appetite. vs Mag 7 is down **-4.3%** this month. Megacap platform tech is traditionally among the highest-beta pockets in the market and should lead in a high-beta environment. — The high-beta leadership is concentrated in semis (**+28.5%**) and quantum (**+29.9%**), not megacap platforms. GOOGL drags Mag 7 lower while the hardware layer runs. Trust the theme data over the factor read: the beta is real, just in a different pocket than you would expect.

MTUM/SPY at z=3.96 (in the top 1% of its yearly range) is the single most stretched reading in the dataset and the most crowded mean-reversion candidate in the factor space. XLK/SPY at z=3.19 (top 2%), SPHB/SPY at z=2.93 (top 1%), and SPHB/SPLV at z=2.66 (top 2%) are running the same concentrated trade: own high-beta momentum tech.

XLC/SPY at z=-3.22 (bottom 1% of its yearly range) is the sharpest negative extreme; it snaps back the moment comms finds a catalyst. Against those, EWT/SPY at z=2.86 (top 1%, +48.42% YTD) and SOXX/SMH at z=3.23 (top 1%) look like confirmed trends anchored in real semiconductor demand.

MCHI/SPY at z=-2.48 (bottom 2%) is trend continuation: do not fade China until you see a policy impulse shift. QQQ/TLT at z=2.64 (top 2%) holds as long as rate cut expectations stay measured.

IWM/SPY +8.4% +2.0σMDY/SPY +4.3% +0.9σRSP/SPY +0.6% -0.4σIWB/SPY -0.4% -2.1σIWM/QQQ -0.8% -0.4σQQQE/QQQ -1.2% -0.8σIWO/IWN -1.4% -0.1σMDY/IWM -3.8% -1.4σRSP/QQQ -8.0% -1.9σ
Rising line = first ticker outperforming the second (relative strength), rebased to 0%. zσ = how stretched the ratio is vs its 1-year mean (|z|≥2 = extreme).

This chart plots nine size-segment relative-strength ratios: a rising line means the numerator is outperforming the denominator.

Small caps are the standout: IWM/SPY leads the basket at +5.7% this month and +8.45% year-to-date; the laggard MDY/IWM sits at -0.93% monthly and -3.81% ytd, meaning mid-caps are losing ground even to small-caps.

The most stretched ratio is IWB/SPY at -2.13 standard deviations below its 1-year mean, confirming that S&P 500 mega-cap concentration has pushed the Russell 1000 large-blend historically far below the index on a relative basis.

If MDY/IWM stays negative while IWM/SPY holds, the small-cap rally is speculative rotation.

Resist reading it as a durable broadening of market internals.

VLUE/SPY +31.6% +2.7σSPHB/SPLV +24.7% +2.7σMTUM/SPY +19.4% +4.0σSPHB/SPY +18.0% +2.9σIVW/IVE +4.0% +1.5σSPYG/SPYV +3.6% +1.4σQUAL/SPY -0.5% -0.7σSIZE/SPY -0.6% -0.9σUSMV/SPY -7.1% -2.0σIWF/IWD -8.5% -1.4σMTUM/VLUE -9.3% -1.3σ
Rising line = first ticker outperforming the second (relative strength), rebased to 0%. zσ = how stretched the ratio is vs its 1-year mean (|z|≥2 = extreme).

Each line compares a factor tilt to another as a relative-strength ratio: a rising SPHB/SPLV means high-beta stocks are outpacing low-volatility stocks, a direct read on risk appetite.

Risk appetite is wide open: SPHB/SPLV leads at +12.7% this month and +24.73% year-to-date, while IWF/IWD (growth vs value) is the laggard at -4.9% monthly and -8.53% ytd; high-beta leading as value beats growth tells you the rally is running on cyclical and rate-sensitive stocks.

The danger sits in momentum: MTUM/SPY is +3.96 standard deviations above its 1-year mean, historically extreme, and a momentum unwind from that level would hit the entire factor complex hard and fast.

If IWF/IWD turns and growth begins recovering while SPHB/SPLV fades, that is your signal the risk-on phase is done and quality is reclaiming the lead.

XLK/SPY +19.8% +3.2σXLE/SPY +9.7% +0.1σXLB/SPY +4.4% +0.1σXLI/SPY +3.2% +0.1σXLRE/SPY +2.3% -0.8σXLP/SPY -0.4% -1.2σXLU/SPY -6.8% -2.1σXLY/SPY -8.5% -2.1σXLF/SPY -10.9% -1.5σXLV/SPY -11.2% -1.5σXLC/SPY -12.9% -3.2σ
Rising line = first ticker outperforming the second (relative strength), rebased to 0%. zσ = how stretched the ratio is vs its 1-year mean (|z|≥2 = extreme).

Each ratio shows a sector's performance against the S&P 500: a rising line means the sector is beating the index.

Technology dominates: XLK/SPY is up +7.17% this month and +19.77% year-to-date, the widest positive spread in the basket, while XLE/SPY has dropped -10.59% this month, cutting deep into a ytd gain that still sits at +9.74%.

The most compressed pair is XLC/SPY at -3.22 standard deviations below its 1-year mean, a historically depressed relative reading for communication services.

When XLC/SPY starts recovering from that extreme, treat it as an early signal that growth leadership is broadening beyond hardware and software into the ad and media complex.

XLK/XLU +28.5% +3.1σXLK/XLP +20.3% +2.5σXLE/XLU +17.8% +1.1σXLI/XLU +10.7% +2.5σXLB/XLP +4.9% +1.6σXLI/XLP +3.6% +1.7σXLY/XLU -1.8% +0.2σXLF/XLU -4.4% +0.2σXLY/XLP -8.1% -0.4σXLF/XLP -10.6% -0.4σ
Rising line = first ticker outperforming the second (relative strength), rebased to 0%. zσ = how stretched the ratio is vs its 1-year mean (|z|≥2 = extreme).

This basket pairs each cyclical sector against a defensive counterpart: a rising line means the cyclical is winning the pair, confirming risk appetite.

Technology is the dominant cyclical: XLK/XLP leads at +10.08% this month and +20.3% year-to-date, while the energy pair XLE/XLU has collapsed -9.7% this month from a ytd level that still sits at +17.76%.

The most extreme ratio is XLK/XLU at +3.07 standard deviations above its 1-year mean, placing tech's lead over utilities at a one-year extreme.

XLE/XLU's sharp reversal while XLK/XLU holds at that stretch is the divergence to track: commodity cyclicals cracking first often precedes the tech-defensive spread beginning to close, and the regime shift starts there.

EMB/TLT +1.7% +1.8σTIP/IEF +1.6% +0.8σHYG/IEF +1.1% +1.4σLQD/IEF +0.9% +1.7σMBB/TLT +0.6% +1.1σTLT/IEF +0.5% -0.4σJNK/TLT +0.5% +0.9σSHY/TLT +0.3% +0.7σAGG/TLT +0.3% +1.0σHYG/LQD +0.2% +0.5σBKLN/HYG -1.9% -1.3σ
Rising line = first ticker outperforming the second (relative strength), rebased to 0%. zσ = how stretched the ratio is vs its 1-year mean (|z|≥2 = extreme).

Each line here plots how one fixed-income asset performs versus another, with a rising line meaning the numerator is outperforming the denominator.

Duration is winning right now.

TLT/IEF (20-year vs 7-to-10-year Treasuries) leads this month at +1.87% and +0.54% year to date, meaning the long end of the curve is getting bid while the rest of the world is chasing risk.

SHY/TLT fell -2.78% this month, the worst in the basket, confirming short maturities are losing ground to long ones and the curve is being pulled from the back end.

The most stretched pair is EMB/TLT at 1.81 standard deviations above its one-year average, meaning emerging market debt has moved unusually far ahead of Treasuries.

When EMB/TLT rolls over from this level, that is your first read on credit stress broadening, and it will show up here before equity volatility does.

QQQ/TLT +22.0% +2.6σQQQ/GLD +19.8% +1.3σSPY/IEF +12.1% +2.4σSPY/TLT +11.5% +2.2σSPY/GLD +9.6% +0.6σSPY/UUP +7.1% +1.9σGLD/TLT +1.8% +0.4σTLT/GLD -1.7% -0.5σHYG/SPY -9.9% -2.1σSPY/DBC -12.5% -0.4σ
Rising line = first ticker outperforming the second (relative strength), rebased to 0%. zσ = how stretched the ratio is vs its 1-year mean (|z|≥2 = extreme).

These ratios track how equities compare with bonds, commodities, and real assets, with a rising line signaling that risk appetite is expanding.

The standout move is SPY/DBC (US equities vs a broad commodity basket) surging +13.39% this month, closing much of a -12.53% year-to-date deficit where commodities had led.

QQQ/TLT is the most stretched pairing, sitting 2.64 standard deviations above its one-year average, with tech equities trading at an unusually wide premium to long bonds.

GLD/TLT lagged at -7.0% this month, with gold losing ground to bonds: the market is reducing its inflation hedge, which confirms the equity rally is being read as disinflationary.

With QQQ/TLT this far extended, the sequence to watch is gold stabilizing versus bonds first, then bonds starting to close on equities, which would mark the beginning of a genuine risk-off rotation rather than a one-day pullback.

USO/UNG +86.3% +1.2σUSO/GLD +72.9% +1.2σGLD/SLV +2.8% -0.8σGDX/GLD +0.0% +0.6σGDXJ/GDX -0.0% +0.6σSLV/GLD -2.7% +0.8σURA/SPY -4.5% -0.8σBNO/USO -6.9% -2.2σGDX/SPY -8.7% -0.1σPPLT/GLD -15.6% -0.1σPALL/GLD -17.0% -1.0σDBA/DBC -18.6% -1.4σ
Rising line = first ticker outperforming the second (relative strength), rebased to 0%. zσ = how stretched the ratio is vs its 1-year mean (|z|≥2 = extreme).

Each ratio here compares one commodity to another, and a rising line means the first is pulling ahead of the second in price performance.

Gold is asserting dominance.

GLD/SLV leads at +4.04% this month and +2.76% year to date, with gold outperforming silver.

Silver carries more industrial demand than gold, so when gold consistently wins the pair, the market is favoring the monetary store of value over cyclical growth demand.

USO/UNG (crude oil vs natural gas) fell -17.64% this month, a sharp reversal from a +86.33% year-to-date lead, meaning oil has given back ground to nat gas rapidly after dominating for months.

The most stretched reading is BNO/USO at -2.23 standard deviations below its one-year average, with Brent crude unusually cheap relative to WTI.

Brent at an unusual discount to US crude signals weak demand at the international benchmark, and a snapback in BNO/USO would be your first sign that the global oil bid is returning.

XLP/SPY -0.4% -1.2σGLD/UUP -2.3% +0.2σUUP/SPY -6.6% -1.8σXLU/SPY -6.8% -2.1σUSMV/SPY -7.1% -2.0σGLD/SPY -8.8% -0.6σTLT/SPY -10.3% -2.1σVIXY/SPY -20.4% -1.5σ
Rising line = first ticker outperforming the second (relative strength), rebased to 0%. zσ = how stretched the ratio is vs its 1-year mean (|z|≥2 = extreme).

This basket measures how safe-haven assets perform relative to equities, with a rising TLT/SPY line meaning bonds are outperforming stocks and protection is being sought.

The haven bid is essentially absent.

TLT/SPY sits -2.12 standard deviations below its one-year average, the most stretched pair in this basket, and the so-called leader at just +0.97% this month still carries a -10.34% year-to-date deficit.

VIXY/SPY (volatility vs equities) is down -17.51% this month and -20.41% year to date, meaning the market is pricing almost no fear at all.

The setup to watch: if TLT/SPY posts a second consecutive month of gains and VIXY/SPY stops making new lows, that sequence marks the early rotation into protection, and with QQQ/TLT in the cross-asset basket stretched at 2.64 standard deviations, the unwind when it arrives will be fast.

EWY/SPY +85.0% +2.4σEWT/SPY +48.4% +2.9σEEM/EFA +14.9% +2.7σEEM/SPY +12.3% +2.0σEWJ/SPY +4.9% +0.4σEFA/SPY -2.2% -1.1σVWO/VEA -3.5% -1.3σEWG/EFA -8.1% -1.5σEWZ/EEM -11.0% -2.1σMCHI/SPY -19.7% -2.5σINDA/EEM -27.0% -1.8σFXI/EEM -28.6% -2.2σ
Rising line = first ticker outperforming the second (relative strength), rebased to 0%. zσ = how stretched the ratio is vs its 1-year mean (|z|≥2 = extreme).

Each line plots an international equity ETF's return relative to a benchmark, usually SPY or a peer region ETF, so a rising line means that country is outperforming US equities and a falling line means it is losing ground.

Korea (EWY/SPY) leads at +16.08% this month and +84.96% year-to-date, a combination of dollar weakness, compressed valuations, and a heavy semiconductor tilt in Korean indexes that has produced one of the strongest single-country relative moves in the basket.

Brazil relative to EM (EWZ/EEM) is the weakest at -9.9% for the month and -11.01% YTD, confirming that commodity-heavy Latin American exposure is losing share even inside a rising EM universe.

Taiwan (EWT/SPY) sits 2.86 standard deviations above its 1-year mean, the most extended reading in the basket, which makes it a reversion candidate rather than a momentum add at current levels.

If EWY/SPY rolls over while EWT/SPY holds near 2.86 sigma above its mean, the international rotation is concentrating into fewer positions, and that narrowing breadth inside the basket is typically a late-stage signal for the trade.

SMH/XLK +30.2% +2.1σOIH/XLE +17.4% +1.7σKRE/XLF +15.7% +1.7σSOXX/SMH +15.0% +3.2σXBI/XLV +13.6% +1.3σKBE/XLF +12.6% +1.7σXHB/XLY +5.0% +0.2σIBB/XLV +3.2% +0.7σXRT/XLY +2.5% +1.3σXOP/XLE +1.6% -0.5σITB/XLY +1.0% -0.6σXME/XLB +0.3% +0.7σIGV/XLK -31.9% -1.8σ
Rising line = first ticker outperforming the second (relative strength), rebased to 0%. zσ = how stretched the ratio is vs its 1-year mean (|z|≥2 = extreme).

Each line measures an industry sub-sector ETF's return relative to its parent sector benchmark, so a rising line means the sub-industry is beating the broader sector and a falling line means it is lagging.

Homebuilders relative to consumer discretionary (XHB/XLY) leads at +11.87% this month and +4.96% YTD, a divergence that tells you rate-sensitive housing demand is holding up even as broader consumer discretionary stalls.

Software relative to tech (IGV/XLK) is the biggest underperformer at -9.12% for the month and -31.94% YTD, a sustained drawdown confirming the market is rotating within technology toward hardware and away from software multiples.

Semiconductor equipment (SOXX/SMH) is the most stretched at 3.23 standard deviations above its 1-year mean; at that extension the near-term risk is reversion, not continuation.

A breakdown in XHB/XLY alongside a recovery in IGV/XLK would tell you that rate expectations have shifted enough to flip the leadership regime inside both consumer and tech simultaneously.

ETHA/IBIT -21.7% -1.1σGBTC/GLD -26.5% -1.0σIBIT/SPY -32.6% -1.5σBITO/SPY -33.9% -1.3σIBIT/QQQ -38.4% -1.7σ
Rising line = first ticker outperforming the second (relative strength), rebased to 0%. zσ = how stretched the ratio is vs its 1-year mean (|z|≥2 = extreme).

Each line here compares a crypto ETF's return to a benchmark, so a rising line means that asset is gaining ground on its reference and this basket tells you whether crypto is winning or losing the capital flow battle against equities and between crypto assets.

Every member is in negative territory for the month: the leader ETHA/IBIT (Ethereum relative to Bitcoin) is down -1.43% this month and -21.73% YTD, meaning Ethereum is losing to Bitcoin even in the best-performing slot of a uniformly weak basket.

Bitcoin relative to QQQ (IBIT/QQQ) is the worst at -16.94% for the month and -38.35% YTD, and at -1.66 standard deviations below its 1-year mean it represents Bitcoin underperforming Nasdaq tech by a historically wide discount.

Until IBIT/QQQ stabilizes and begins recovering toward its mean, you are watching crypto lose the asset allocation competition to growth equities, which tells you institutional capital is rotating away from Bitcoin and back into tech.

A recovery in IBIT/QQQ back above its 1-year mean would be the first credible confirmation that crypto is re-entering the risk-on rotation.

XLK +32.0% +2.9σXLE +20.9% +0.9σXLB +15.1% +1.8σXLI +13.7% +2.0σXLRE +12.7% +2.7σXLP +9.7% +1.2σXLU +2.7% +0.3σXLY +0.8% +0.6σXLF -1.9% +0.9σXLV -2.2% +0.7σXLC -4.1% -0.4σ

This chart rebases each SPDR sector ETF to 0% at the start of the period, so you are reading absolute return dispersion across 11 sectors: the wider the spread between lines, the more differentiated the macro regime underneath the index.

Technology (XLK) is the runaway leader at +9.22% this month and +31.98% year-to-date, compounding a lead that has widened across 2026 rather than giving back ground.

Energy (XLE) is the worst for the month at -8.88%, yet holds +20.92% for the year, so the selloff is sharp and recent rather than a full reversal of the 2026 trend.

Communication services (XLC) is down -4.12% this month and -4.05% for the year, the only other sector negative on both horizons alongside energy, and a tech rally that cannot lift XLC is narrower than the headline return implies.

Watch healthcare (XLV at +4.41% monthly but -2.16% YTD): if that month-to-date bounce extends into positive YTD territory, the market is rotating into defensive growth alongside tech leadership, a configuration that warrants tighter position sizing in momentum-heavy tech.

GOOGL +17.3% +1.4σNVDA +11.2% +1.5σAMZN +8.9% +0.9σAAPL +8.3% +1.4σTSLA -5.3% +0.4σMETA -9.6% -1.5σMSFT -16.3% -1.4σ

This chart tracks seven large-cap US technology and consumer-internet stocks rebased to zero: a rising line means the group is advancing collectively and a falling line means it is ceding ground.

The basket is fracturing: TSLA leads the month at +1.17% but is -5.31% year-to-date, and GOOGL lags at -6.88% for June despite holding +17.28% for the year.

XLK added +9.22% this month while the top performer in the Mag 7 managed just +1.17%, which tells you the tech rally has rotated into AI hardware and away from mega-cap internet rather than lifting this group.

A GOOGL recovery above its June high is the one data point that flips this read; continued weakness confirms the rotation is structural.

MU +235.8% +3.2σMRVL +224.5% +3.9σAMD +144.8% +3.3σAMAT +117.6% +3.1σLRCX +109.0% +2.9σKLAC +102.8% +3.5σASML +61.0% +2.4σTSM +37.2% +2.1σAVGO +13.3% +1.1σNVDA +11.2% +1.5σSMCI +2.5% -0.7σ

This chart tracks eleven semiconductor companies rebased to zero, so the line captures collective movement across the group since the base date.

Dispersion inside the basket is extreme: MRVL leads with +71.69% in a single month and +224.45% year-to-date, while AVGO is down -6.39% this month and up only +13.29% for the year.

Those numbers make the basket a stock-selection exercise rather than a sector call, because MRVL's gain reflects an AI networking win specific to that company while AVGO's slip reflects a different positioning in the AI supply chain.

Watch for AVGO and the broader group to start closing the gap: if they do, it signals a true cyclical broadening in semis; if MRVL holds its lead alone, the ETF is flattering the group's health.

DDOG +73.4% +2.7σPANW +54.3% +2.9σCRWD +49.4% +2.6σNET +19.2% +1.5σMDB -13.3% +0.4σSNOW -21.2% -0.9σNOW -28.7% -1.1σCRM -33.9% -1.8σADBE -36.9% -2.0σZS -41.2% -1.5σTEAM -42.2% -1.0σHUBS -50.3% -1.5σ

This chart tracks twelve software companies rebased to zero, showing their cumulative return since the base date.

The dominant feature right now is earnings-driven divergence: NET leads at +15.77% for the month and +19.15% year-to-date, while ZS has lost -25.81% this month and -41.25% year-to-date.

A spread of that size means the market is pricing individual quarterly prints and the group average is meaningless as a signal; you need to own the right stock, not the basket.

The split tells you the market is paying for AI-adjacent networking and cybersecurity and penalizing security vendors that lack an AI-driven growth story, so watch ZS for a base, because stabilization there would signal the worst of the reset is behind the group.

DDOG +73.4% +2.7σPANW +54.3% +2.9σCRWD +49.4% +2.6σNET +19.2% +1.5σMDB -13.3% +0.4σFDS -16.0% -0.7σSNOW -21.2% -0.9σPYPL -27.2% -1.4σNOW -28.7% -1.1σCRM -33.9% -1.8σWDAY -36.7% -1.3σADBE -36.9% -2.0σZS -41.2% -1.5σTEAM -42.2% -1.0σHUBS -50.3% -1.5σINTU -55.5% -1.9σ

This chart tracks sixteen SaaS companies rebased to zero and shows how the group has moved collectively since the base date.

The basket is splitting: NET leads at +15.77% for the month and +19.15% year-to-date, while INTU has lost -30.53% this month and -55.51% year-to-date.

That spread tells you the market is sorting SaaS into AI-enabled platforms and legacy software facing disruption risk on pricing, and the group average is no longer a useful positioning signal.

The regime call is that AI-adjacent cloud infrastructure (NET, +19.15% YTD) still commands a premium while legacy SaaS faces a structural repricing, and INTU's -55.51% year-to-date is the warning sign for other platforms with similar profiles.

MU +235.8% +3.2σMRVL +224.5% +3.9σARM +223.7% +3.6σDELL +209.1% +3.5σAMD +144.8% +3.3σVRT +80.9% +1.6σTSM +37.2% +2.1σANET +23.3% +1.6σAVGO +13.3% +1.1σNVDA +11.2% +1.5σSMCI +2.5% -0.7σ

This basket tracks the hardware and infrastructure layer powering AI compute: chip architects, semiconductor designers, and data center equipment makers, all rebased to zero so a rising line means the group is gaining in price as a bloc.

ARM is the runaway leader, up +72.65% in one month and +223.72% year-to-date, while VRT has given back -6.49% this month despite a +80.91% YTD gain.

The spread between those two is the lesson: the market is rewarding chip IP and silicon architects over physical data center build-out, a rotation inside the theme rather than a broad exit.

If VRT keeps lagging while ARM accelerates, AI capex optimism is concentrating into the highest-margin, lowest-capital-intensity companies, a pattern that historically precedes a broader sector pullback even as the index keeps printing highs.

LMT +6.8% +0.2σGD +4.7% +1.2σLHX +0.5% +0.1σBA +0.1% +0.6σRTX -2.0% +0.4σNOC -7.8% -0.9σHII -14.6% -0.4σPLTR -21.8% -1.4σAXON -22.0% -1.1σ

This basket holds traditional and next-generation defense contractors, all rebased to zero; a rising line means the group is gaining in price as a bloc.

AXON leads at +10.01% this month but sits -22.02% YTD, while HII is the clearest drag at -9.26% in June and -14.55% year-to-date.

The lesson: the market is paying for technology-forward defense and still punishing shipbuilding and legacy platforms, which means the budget bid inside this basket is narrow and not broad-based.

If HII cannot stabilize, the sector is telling you that headline spending increases are fully priced into the theme but the contracts are not flowing where traditional platforms expected, and a basket that cannot broaden beyond one or two names does not last.

DNN +9.9% +0.5σCCJ +8.9% +0.6σURA +5.3% +0.2σNXE +3.6% +0.5σUEC -6.9% -0.2σOKLO -21.2% -0.7σSMR -32.5% -1.1σLEU -34.5% -1.0σ

This basket tracks uranium miners and small modular reactor developers, all rebased to zero; a rising line signals the market is pricing in higher uranium demand or nuclear power policy tailwinds.

SMR leads at +5.07% this month but is still down -32.49% year-to-date, while UEC is the laggard at -7.54% in June and -6.90% YTD.

Even the strongest stock in the basket is down more than -32% for the year, which tells you the 2024 nuclear thesis has been repriced sharply lower across every company in the group.

Watch whether SMR-specific catalysts (reactor licensing, utility contracts) can pull UEC and the rest of the basket along; if UEC keeps falling while SMR bounces, the bid stays isolated and the uranium bull thesis is not broadening.

AMGN +7.8% +1.0σLLY +3.5% +1.5σNVO -15.3% -0.7σVKTX -18.0% -0.1σ

This basket tracks the two dominant GLP-1 obesity drug developers, rebased to zero; a rising line means the market is expanding its estimate of the drug class's revenue trajectory and pricing power.

LLY leads at +13.17% this month and +3.50% YTD, while NVO is nearly flat at +0.18% in June and down -15.33% year-to-date.

LLY's +3.50% YTD against NVO's -15.33% tells you the market has stopped treating GLP-1 as a theme trade and started picking a winner: own LLY specifically, do not own the basket broadly.

LLY's strength is pulling healthcare (XLV up +4.41% this month) and is one of the few domestically-driven growth stories working right now; a reversal in LLY would pressure the entire sector, so that single stock is the tell.

IONQ +33.8% +1.3σQUBT +1.5% -0.4σRGTI -1.7% +0.2σQBTS -7.3% +0.6σARQQ -31.8% -0.9σ

This basket plots five quantum computing stocks rebased to zero percent, so a rising line means the group is generating positive returns from its common starting date.

RGTI leads with +39.53% in one month despite sitting -1.74% year-to-date, which marks this as a recovery bounce rather than a sustained trend: the best performer in the basket spent most of the year underwater before this month's run.

QUBT, the laggard, still gained +14.98% on the month, telling you that speculative risk appetite is broad enough to lift every stock in the group at once.

When the leader is still negative year-to-date after a +39.53% monthly gain, the base is damaged and the move is fragile: watch for RGTI to stall, because if it does, the whole basket reverses hard and fast.

FTNT +85.8% +3.2σPANW +54.3% +2.9σCRWD +49.4% +2.6σOKTA +39.1% +2.5σNET +19.2% +1.5σS +1.7% -0.6σZS -41.2% -1.5σ

This basket tracks seven cybersecurity stocks rebased to zero percent, with a rising line meaning the group as a whole is profitable from its starting price.

The internal split is the defining fact: OKTA has gained +33.66% this month and +39.10% year-to-date, while ZS has lost -25.81% in the past month and -41.25% since January, putting the two stocks on opposite ends of every performance table that exists.

Those two stocks are trading on completely different fundamentals, and owning the basket at the index level means you simultaneously hold the best-performing and the worst-performing cyber story in a single position.

ZS is your tell for the basket's direction: if it stabilizes, the group becomes more constructive; if the selling continues, ZS will cap basket-level returns no matter how much further OKTA advances.

XYZ +12.4% +0.7σAFRM -6.1% +0.2σHOOD -13.1% -0.1σNU -26.2% -1.3σPYPL -27.2% -1.4σCOIN -27.4% -1.2σSOFI -37.0% -1.0σBILL -50.7% -1.6σ

This basket holds eight fintech stocks rebased to zero percent, and a rising line means the group as a whole is above its common starting price.

HOOD leads with +29.76% on the month but remains -13.11% year-to-date, flagging this as a bounce off a broken base rather than a genuine trend reversal: the month's move only partially repairs a full year of damage.

BILL, the laggard, is down -11.21% this month and -50.68% for the year, and at that level of drawdown a recovery requires a full fundamental re-rating of the business rather than a change in sentiment.

The lesson this basket teaches is to size by individual stock, because the spread between HOOD's year-to-date and BILL's year-to-date is so extreme that any position built on basket-level exposure dilutes the best trade while still carrying the worst.

MRNA +65.3% +1.3σBIIB +10.3% +1.3σAMGN +7.8% +1.0σGILD +1.4% -0.2σVRTX -2.4% +0.1σREGN -21.1% -0.6σALNY -29.4% -1.4σ

This basket tracks seven biotech stocks rebased to zero percent, with a rising line meaning the group is gaining from its common starting date.

The moves here are the most contained of any basket today: AMGN leads with +8.90% in one month and +7.82% year-to-date, while GILD lags with -4.87% on the month but still holds a +1.44% gain for the year, leaving the spread between leader and laggard narrow by the standards of any other theme.

That contained spread and modest absolute gains match what XLV itself is doing, up +4.41% on the month: the bid in large-cap biotech is disciplined and anchored on earnings quality, with speculative positioning absent from the move.

AMGN is your proxy for the group: a reversal below its monthly gains would signal that the defensive growth bid is running out of fuel and that rotation back toward higher-beta sectors is underway.

GOLD +31.6% +0.2σAU +9.9% +0.6σNEM +6.1% +0.7σWPM +5.5% +0.4σFNV +4.7% +0.2σAEM +1.9% +0.1σKGC -2.6% +0.2σ

This chart plots 7 gold mining stocks rebased to zero percent, so a rising line means the basket is appreciating in absolute price terms from the start date.

GOLD leads at +15.33% this month and +31.55% year-to-date; WPM is the laggard at -3.94% for the month with only +5.53% YTD.

That spread is the story: royalty companies are being discounted relative to direct producers, which tells you this is a stock-specific trade inside the sector rather than a macro gold call lifting every position equally.

Watch WPM: if it starts recovering toward GOLD's pace, that signals genuine broadening conviction in the gold trade and gives the basket a cleaner read-through to the broader safe-haven regime.

C +20.5% +2.5σMS +20.5% +2.6σGS +19.5% +2.5σKRE +13.5% +2.2σPNC +13.1% +2.1σUSB +9.9% +1.9σTFC +3.9% +1.4σBAC +1.1% +1.8σJPM -0.0% +1.8σWFC -10.8% +0.3σ

This chart tracks 10 bank stocks rebased to zero percent, measuring absolute price performance since the period start.

Citigroup leads at +16.88% this month and +20.53% YTD; JPMorgan is the laggard at +8.18% and flat at -0.05% YTD.

The rotation from JPM to C inside the same sector tells you the market is paying for restructuring and catch-up stories right now, not for defensive mega-cap quality.

XLF as the sector ETF is up only +4.17% this month with -1.88% YTD, so the index understates the dispersion inside: C's outperformance is doing work that JPM is not.

If JPM closes the gap to C, that is evidence that bank breadth is genuinely broadening rather than one ticker pulling the weight, and the financials trade becomes easier to size.

MPC +52.5% +1.8σSLB +37.7% +1.5σPSX +31.7% +1.3σHAL +30.1% +1.2σOXY +26.1% +0.8σEOG +21.6% +0.8σWMB +16.6% +0.9σCOP +15.3% +0.7σCVX +14.5% +0.6σXOM +12.7% +0.5σ

This chart shows 10 energy stocks rebased to zero percent, and every member is down this month, making it the weakest corner of the market right now.

MPC is the best performer in the basket and still trails at -2.94%; XOM leads the decline at -13.88%.

XLE as the sector ETF is down -8.88% this month, the worst monthly reading of any sector in the current data, even as it holds +20.92% YTD.

The lesson: year-to-date gains offer no cushion when a positioning flush starts.

MPC is up +52.54% year-to-date and XOM +12.69%, yet both are sold hard.

Watch the MPC-to-XOM spread: if MPC's relative strength collapses toward XOM's, the refiner premium has fully unwound and the entire sector is waiting for a new fundamental catalyst before it stabilizes.

CAT +57.7% +2.0σDE +25.6% +1.2σETN +24.9% +1.6σROK +17.6% +2.3σUNP +17.0% +1.9σHON +15.6% +0.6σEMR +8.6% +1.3σGE +7.7% +2.1σPH +4.1% +0.9σ

This chart plots 9 industrial stocks rebased to zero percent, so a rising line means the basket is gaining in nominal price terms since the start date.

GE leads at +20.84% this month and +7.75% YTD; UNP is the laggard at -1.4% for the month despite +16.97% YTD.

GE is an aerospace engine story and UNP is a freight and economic throughput story, and they are diverging sharply inside an index that looks healthy on the surface.

XLI as the sector ETF posts +5.17% this month and +13.67% YTD, but GE is doing most of the heavy lifting.

If UNP stays weak while GE keeps running, the sector's strength is concentrated in defense and aerospace, not in broad economic activity, and that is a more cautious macro signal than the headline sector number alone suggests.

Source: Polygon EOD · ~/.screener_cache The Philosopher Investor
